title: Model-View-Controller
description:
MVC architecture organizes interactive systems by separating responsibilities into three main components, aiming to isolate business logic from the user interface and application control flow. This separation promotes a modular structure and facilitates system evolution over time. Its organization is based on three conceptual blocks:
[ul]
[b]Model[/b]: Responsible for representing the application's data and business logic. Manages system state and defines domain rules, being independent of the user interface.
[b]View[/b]: Presentation layer responsible for displaying data to the user. Obtains information from the model and renders it appropriately, and multiple views can exist for the same model.
[b]Controller[/b]: Acts as an intermediary between Model and View. Receives user input, interprets those actions, and coordinates updates in the model and the view.[/ul]
This architecture offers advantages such as: [b]high usability[/b], as the separation allows multiple interfaces and facilitates adaptation of the presentation to the user; and [b]good maintainability[/b], since changes in one layer (especially the interface) can be made with reduced impact on the others.
A disadvantage is [b]low compatibility[/b], because the rigid separation between components can hinder integration with legacy systems or architectures that do not follow the same structural pattern.
concept: Separation between data (Model), interface (View), and input logic (Controller).
advantage: High Usability. Allows creating multiple interfaces for the same data with ease.
disadvantage: Low Compatibility. Difficult to integrate with systems that do not follow this rigid pattern.

bullet_point.bullet1: Divides system into Model, View, Controller
bullet_point.bullet2: Controller mediates between user and application
bullet_point.bullet3: Views display data from the model
